---
title: "Eggs"
description: |
  Which came first, the chicken or the egg?
date: '2026-02-23'
image: "egg.jpg"
categories:
    - Food
format: html
toc: true
lightbox: true
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  error: false
  message: false
---


```{r}
library(here)
source(here("packages.R"))
source(here("functions.R"))

series_filter = 8

data = pollresults(series_filter)
```

```{r}
simple_bar_question(data, "In a pan")
```

```{r}
simple_bar_question(data, "In a pot")
```

```{r}
simple_bar_question(data, "Yolk")
```

```{r}
simple_bar_question(data, "Condiment")
```

```{r}
simple_bar_question(data, "Sandwich")
```

# How decisive were these results?

```{r}
#| label: summary-stats
summary_eggs = summarize_two_option(data)
```

**TL;DR:** the least controversial opinion of the week is that Egg Salad Sandwiches lose — Breakfast Sandwich won `r summary_eggs %>% filter(question == "Sandwich") %>% pull(leader_votes)`–`r summary_eggs %>% filter(question == "Sandwich") %>% pull(trailer_votes)` (`r summary_eggs %>% filter(question == "Sandwich") %>% pull(leader_share) %>% percent(1)`). On the flip side, "In a pot" was a genuine dead heat: Poached and Hard/Soft Boiled tied `r summary_eggs %>% filter(question == "In a pot") %>% pull(leader_votes)`–`r summary_eggs %>% filter(question == "In a pot") %>% pull(trailer_votes)`, which is as close to a coin flip as poll math allows.

```{r}
#| label: summary-table
binom_summary_table(summary_eggs)
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-leader-ci
#| fig-cap: "Leader shares with 95% confidence intervals."
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 3.8
leader_ci_plot(summary_eggs)
```

# Do the spice-and-mess people cluster together?

Runny yolks are messier, and hot sauce is a mess-tolerant condiment — so do the same people gravitate toward both? We didn't ask people to link their two answers, but Yolk and Condiment happened to draw the exact same number of respondents (`r data %>% filter(question == "Yolk") %>% pull(N) %>% unique()` each), so the overlap bound is unusually tight this week.

```{r}
#| label: overlap-yolk-condiment
ov_yolk_sauce = overlap_bounds(
  a_total = data %>% filter(question == "Yolk", option == "Runny Yolk") %>% pull(votes),
  N_a     = data %>% filter(question == "Yolk") %>% pull(N) %>% unique(),
  b_total = data %>% filter(question == "Condiment", option == "with Hot Sauce") %>% pull(votes),
  N_b     = data %>% filter(question == "Condiment") %>% pull(N) %>% unique()
)
```

Somewhere between `r ov_yolk_sauce$min_overlap` and `r ov_yolk_sauce$max_overlap` of the `r ov_yolk_sauce$m` overlapping voters (`r percent(ov_yolk_sauce$min_overlap/ov_yolk_sauce$m, 1)`–`r percent(ov_yolk_sauce$max_overlap/ov_yolk_sauce$m, 1)`) are both Runny Yolk *and* Hot Sauce people, versus about `r round(ov_yolk_sauce$expected_overlap)` (`r percent(ov_yolk_sauce$expected_overlap/ov_yolk_sauce$m, 1)`) expected if yolk texture and condiment choice are unrelated. Since both options are individually so popular, the floor on their overlap is high no matter what — the "messy egg" coalition is large by construction.
